retrieved_docs.title: Autoimmune Pancreatitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Although corticosteroid therapy induces remission in as many as 99%, [36] relapse or steroid dependence can occur in up to one-third of patients, especially in IgG4-related AIP. These patients may require long-term steroids or steroid-sparing agents. [20] [37] If there is no response to steroid treatment after 4 to 6 weeks, pancreatic cancer must be excluded. If the diagnosis of AIP is confirmed, an immunomodulator such as azathioprine can facilitate steroid tapering, [38] , though it is not effective as monotherapy. Rituximab is a second-line therapy for type 1 AIP in patients with a contraindication to glucocorticoids or if a complete course of steroid therapy is not feasible. [39] Colchicine and various biologic therapies can be effective for both treatment and maintenance therapy, primarily in type 2 AIP. [28] [40] Finally, plasmacytoid dendritic cells and signaling pathways mediated by type 1 interferon and interleukin-33 are targets for novel therapies. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Dosing in both clinical trials involved eight infusions over six months. However, the duration and total infusion numbers are debatable, as an improvement has been reported by injection 2 or 3. Smith et al. showed that the therapeutic effects were rapid, with 43% of patients in the teprotumumab group and 4% of patients in the placebo group responding at week 6. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Teprotumumab -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with severe disease who continue demonstrating response at 24 weeks to teprotumumab can be considered for additional infusions using the same dosing schedule. [56] In contrast, early discontinuation should be considered if there is no improvement at the 4th to 6th infusion.

retrieved_docs.title: Andexanet Alfa -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the indications for the use of andexanet alfa. Assess the dosing parameters for andexanet alfa. Evaluate the mechanism of action of andexanet alfa. Implement effective collaboration and communication among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes and treatment efficacy for patients who might benefit from andexanet alfa therapy. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Anifrolumab  -- Administration -- Specific Patient Population
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding women: No information is available on the clinical use of anifrolumab during breastfeeding. As anifrolumab is a large protein molecule, the amount in milk is likely to be very low. Anifroluman may be destroyed in the infant's gastrointestinal tract and is probably minimally absorbed. [21] Pediatric patients: No dose adjustment information is available for pediatric patients as no safety and efficacy were evaluated for patients younger than 18.

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management -- Second Relapse or Subsequent Relapse
retrieved_docs.content: In 2022, the FDA approved teclistamab, the first bispecific T-cell engager antibody against CD38 expressed on the myeloma cells, for patients with relapsed or refractory multiple myeloma who have failed 4 prior lines of therapy. Belantamab mafodotin is anti-BCMA humanized immunoglobulin G (IgG) antibody conjugated with monomethyl auristatin. The drug received accelerated approval in the US for patients who had progressed on at least 4 lines of therapy. It is being withdrawn from the market based on the results of randomized phase 3 trials (DREAMM-3), as it did not show clinical benefit compared to pomalidomide and dexamethasone.

retrieved_docs.title: Relapsed and Refractory Multiple Myeloma -- Treatment / Management -- First Relapse
retrieved_docs.content: For patients with lenalidomide-refractory MM, future regimens are influenced by whether the disease is refractory to bortezomib and/or daratumumab. The disease, which is refractory to both lenalidomide and bortezomib, can respond to a different proteasome inhibitor like carfilzomib or a different immunomodulator like pomalidomide in combination with dexamethasone and daratumumab. For patients with lenalidomide-refractory MM, treatment options include daratumumab, bortezomib, and dexamethasone (DVd); daratumumab, pomalidomide, and dexamethasone (DPd); bortezomib, pomalidomide and dexamethasone (VPd); or isatuximab, pomalidomide, and dexamethasone (IsaPd).

retrieved_docs.title: Glatiramer -- Administration -- Specific Patient Population
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfee ding considerations: Glatiramer is undetectable in most women and appears in only low concentration for up to 3 hours in others. Additionally, maternal use of glatiramer does not cause adverse effects in breastfed infants. Absorption by the infant is estimated to be minimal (<3%). Most experts consider glatiramer safe and a preferred disease-modifying agent for managing multiple sclerosis during lactation. For additional safety, breastfeeding could be withheld for 1 to 3 hours after a dose to prevent infant exposure. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Pancreatic Islet Cell Cancer(Archived) -- Medical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: Everolimus bind with the mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR) to inhibit it. Sunitinib is a multiple receptor tyrosine kinase inhibitor. Both of these drugs are a second-line treatment for patients with progressive pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors. Both of the drugs have shown a significant increase in the progression-free survival and overall survival of the patients. [30] [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Nivolumab -- Administration -- Specific Patient Population
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding considerations: No data exists regarding the presence of nivolumab in breast milk. However, due to the potential for severe adverse reactions in breastfeeding children, women are advised not to breastfeed during nivolumab treatment and for 5 months after the final dose.

retrieved_docs.title: Tumor Treatment Fields in Oncology -- Indications -- Pancreatic Cancer
retrieved_docs.content: The utility of TTF is also being studied in pancreatic cancer, particularly advanced-stage carcinomas, due to its antiproliferative effects in preclinical trials. The replicability of these effects is being studied in an ongoing clinical trial, PANOVA, in which patients with pancreatic adenocarcinoma received either TTF along with gemcitabine or gemcitabine and nab-paclitaxel.

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Biosimilars is FDA approved for the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis (RA), polyarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA), psoriatic arthritis (PA), ankylosing spondylitis (AS), plaque psoriasis (PsO), inflammatory bowel disease (IBD): adult Crohn disease (CD), ulcerative colitis (UC), granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA) (Wegener granulomatosis), microscopic polyangiitis (MPA), type 1 diabetes mellitus (DM), non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer, metastatic colorectal cancer, metastatic renal cell carcinoma, glioblastoma, recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer, HER2 (human epidermal growth factor receptor 2) associated breast cancer, and HER2 associated gastric (metastatic) or gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma, chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL). Biosimilars are biotherapeutic agents similar to their respective original biologic product (reference product). This activity will highlight the mechanism of action, adverse event profile, and other key factors pertinent to interprofessional team members in managing patients with inflammatory diseases and the use of biosimilars.

retrieved_docs.title: Biosimilars Use in Medicine for Inflammatory Diseases -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Other indications of biosimilars alongside inflammatory diseases include management for specific malignancies and secondary causes of anemia. In September 2017, bevacizumab-awwb was the first biosimilar that was granted FDA approval for certain cancers. A few months later, in December 2017, biosimilar trastuzumab-dkst was also granted FDA approval for specific malignancies. Cancers indicative of biosimilar therapy include non-squamous non-small cell lung cancer, metastatic colorectal cancer, metastatic renal cell carcinoma, glioblastoma, recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer, HER2 (human epidermal growth factor receptor 2) associated breast cancer, and HER2 associated gastric (metastatic) or gastroesophageal junction adenocarcinoma, chronic lymphocytic leukemia (CLL), non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL). To date, new biosimilars are currently still being FDA approved, with the latest approval coming on July 28, 2021, for biosimilar agent insulin glargine-yfgn, a long-acting insulin agent indicated for pediatric type 1 diabetes mellitus (DM) and adult patients with DM type 1 and DM type 2.

retrieved_docs.content: same antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties. Gilteritinib and midostaurin are recognized for their activity against acute myeloid leukemia (سرطان الدم النخاعي الحاد) by targeting kinases that drive leukemic cell proliferation. The epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitor group, also part of the protein kinase inhibitor and antineoplastic/immunomodulating families, includes canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab, illustrating the common strategy of inhibiting receptor tyrosine kinases to suppress tumor growth. A separate therapeutic branch, hypolipidemic agents, is represented by butoxamine, diallyl trisulfide, the corticosteroid loteprednol, and dietary fiber ispaghula husk (psyllium). Although loteprednol is primarily a steroid, it is included in the hypolipidemic category, highlighting the integration of diverse compounds in lipid‑lowering strategies. Overall, the knowledge network interconnects cholesterol‑lowering drugs, hematologic therapies, BTK and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 764
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. EGFR inhibitors are a subset of protein kinase inhibitors that possess antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, placing them within the broader therapeutic category of agents used to treat cancer and modulate immune responses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 327
retrieved_docs.content: Canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab are both classified as epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors. As members of the EGFR inhibitor class, these agents act on the EGFR signaling pathway, which plays a critical role in the growth and survival of certain cancer cells. Recognizing that both canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab share this mechanism of action helps clinicians select appropriate targeted therapies and anticipate similar pharmacologic effects and potential adverse events associated with EGFR inhibition.

retrieved_docs.content: hydrochloride 1 mg/mL oral solution. Alcohol has an inverse‑isa relationship with heptaminol and the antiretroviral nelfinavir, indicating it does not share their pharmacologic class. Colesevelam is contraindicated in inflammatory pancreatic disorders (non‑diabetic pancreatitis). Clinically, such pancreatic disorders are associated with increased alkalinity of blood and tissues, a metabolic shift toward higher pH that can influence diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic planning. In intravenous therapy, “Amino Acids, I.V. solution additive” includes “Antifibrinolytic amino acids,” whose inverse term “antifibrinolytika” denotes their role in inhibiting fibrinolysis and supporting hemostasis. Collectively, these interconnections illustrate how drug classes, chemical structures, formulation roles, physiological effects and contraindications intersect to inform safe prescribing and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 70
retrieved_docs.content: BCR‑ABL inhibitors, a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating properties, include dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous, imatinib, ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib. Dasatinib, dasatinib anhydrous and imatinib are indicated for lymphoid leukemia, whereas ponatinib, ponatinib hydrochloride and asciminib are indicated for myeloid leukemia. Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors, also protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulating activity, comprise ibrutinib and zanubrutinib; ibrutinib is used to treat chronic lymphocytic leukemia. Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors, another protein kinase inhibitor subclass, consist of canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. By blocking EGFR tyrosine‑kinase signaling, these agents disrupt tumor cell proliferation and survival and can modulate immune responses, providing antineoplastic and immunomodulating effects. Collectively, targeted inhibition of BCR‑ABL, BTK, and EGFR exemplifies precision oncology, linking specific molecular targets to distinct hematologic and solid malignancies and guiding patient‑centered cancer therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 308
retrieved_docs.content: Cykl​ofosfamid, an alkylating chemotherapeutic and immunosuppressive agent, may be used to treat both multiple sclerosis and multiple myeloma, illustrating its therapeutic relevance across a demyelinating autoimmune disorder and a hematologic malignancy. Its capacity to modulate immune activity and inhibit proliferating cells underlies its application in these distinct diseases, emphasizing the importance of versatile treatment options in personalized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 483
retrieved_docs.content: Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors constitute a subclass of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents, and this class includes the drugs ibrutinib and zanubrutinib. Ibrutinib, as a member of the BTK inhibitor group, may be used to treat سرطان الخلايا الليمفاوية المزمن (chronic lymphocytic leukemia). Similarly, Epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) inhibitors are also classified within the broader category of protein kinase inhibitors, antineoplastic and immunomodulating agents; this EGFR inhibitor group comprises agents such as canertinib dihydrochloride and margetuximab. Together, BTK inhibitors and EGFR inhibitors illustrate how distinct targeted therapies share common mechanistic foundations as protein kinase inhibitors with antineoplastic and immunomodulatory effects, underscoring their relevance in the treatment of various cancers and immune‑related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 273
retrieved_docs.content: Abciximab and ibalizumab are both classified as monoklonální protilátky (monoclonal antibodies), indicating that each of these agents belongs to the therapeutic class of antibodies derived from a single immune cell clone and designed to target specific antigens. This shared categorization highlights their role in targeted medical treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 630
retrieved_docs.content: Tanezumab, abciximab and ibalizumab are all classified within the broader category of monoklonální protilátky (monoclonal antibodies). The relationship “isa” indicates that abciximab is a monoclonal antibody, while “mapped_to” shows that ibalizumab is linked to the monoclonal‑antibody class. The “inverse_isa” relationship denotes that monoklonální protilátky constitute the general class to which tanezumab belongs, confirming that tanezumab is a specific monoclonal antibody. Together, these connections highlight that each of these therapeutic agents shares the fundamental characteristic of being monoclonal antibodies, which is important for understanding their mechanisms of action and clinical use.

The monoclonal antibody tanezumab belongs to the broader class of MONOKLONÁLNÍ PROTILÁTKY, while abciximab is a monoclonal antibody used to inhibit platelet aggregation. Collectively, these entities are organized by chemical class, mechanism of action, and clinical indication, providing a comprehensive framework for targeted patient care across viral, bacterial, fungal, oncologic and gastro‑intestinal domains.

Overall, the knowledge network links specific radiopharmaceutical agents (e.g., technetium Tc 99m arcitumomab, technetium Tc 99m depreotide, indium In‑111 pentetreotide) to their molecular origins (monoclonal antibodies, organotechnetium compounds, somatostatin, oligopeptides) and to their functional roles in tumor detection through receptor targeting or chemical composition. This integrated understanding aids clinicians and researchers in selecting appropriate imaging agents based on target biology and chemical class, thereby optimizing diagnostic accuracy and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 0 -- Community 10
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnostic and therapeutic radiopharmaceuticals are linked through oligopeptide scaffolds and radiometal labeling: indium‑111‑labeled statumomab pendetide and acetylphenylalanyl‑prolyl‑boroarginine illustrate peptide‑based agents; indium‑111 pentetreotide binds somatostatin receptors for imaging somatostatin‑receptor‑positive tumours; technetium‑99m‑arcitumomab (derived from monoclonal antibodies) and technetium‑99m‑depreotide (derived from somatostatin peptide) belong to the technetium‑99m tumour‑detection subclass; fluorocholine and the agent ISA are listed among other tumour‑detection radiopharmaceuticals. CP 609754 is specifically linked to technetium‑99m‑arcitumomab. Peptide‑derived scaffolds also connect antiviral drugs: Deltorphin I Ala(2)‑ maps to the protease inhibitor telaprevir, illustrating structural overlap between a peptide fragment and an HIV‑directed agent. Lipopeptidy backbones appear in both antifungal and antiviral agents: the “other antimycotics for systemic use”

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 131
retrieved_docs.content: CP 609754 is linked to technetium Tc‑99m arcitumomab as a more specific entity within that radiopharmaceutical family. Technetium Tc‑99m arcitumomab and technetium Tc‑99m depreotide belong to the class of Technetium‑99m compounds used for tumour detection, which in turn are a subclass of tumour detection radiopharmaceuticals. Both agents are radiolabelled molecules: arcitumomab is derived from monoclonal antibodies (monoklonální protilátky) and from organometallic compounds (organotechneciové sloučeniny), whereas depreotide is derived from the peptide hormone somatostatin. The group “Other diagnostic radiopharmaceuticals for tumour detection in ATC” is itself a type of tumour detection radiopharmaceutical and includes fluorocholine as a member. Fluorocholine is a fluorine‑containing compound (sloučeniny fluoru) that is employed for imaging tumours in anaplastic thyroid carcinoma (ATC). Monoclonal antibodies form a broader category that encompasses specific agents such as abciximab (an isa relationship), ibalizumab (mapped to this class), and tanezumab (an inverse_isa relationship indicating it is a particular monoclonal antibody). Together, these relationships illustrate how diagnostic radiopharmaceuticals are organized by radionuclide label (technetium‑99m), molecular origin (monoclonal antibodies, somatostatin analogues, fluorine compounds), and clinical application (tumour detection in various cancers), providing a structured framework for selecting appropriate imaging agents in patient care.
